prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. My dad started writing again at 70 — this is one of his first pieces in decades. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

- Topic: Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned
- Format: Short personal narrative (~500 words)

Let me analyze:

1. **Clear narrative arc**: The essay has a clear beginning (trying out), middle (failing during tryouts), and end (reflecting on lessons). Good structure.

2. **Specific, vivid details**: "pick and roll," the scrimmage scene, the kid saying "stay out of the way," the list going up on Friday. These details make it feel real and lived-in.

3. **Shows don't just tells**: Rather than saying "I was unprepared," the author shows us through the fast break drill example, not knowing plays, etc.

4. **Authentic voice**: The writing feels natural and age-appropriate, not overwrought. "It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine" - good balance of emotion and reflection.

5. **Multiple layers of learning**: The essay doesn't stop at "I learned I wasn't good at basketball." It goes deeper - the difference between casual and competitive play, how failure redirects you, the concept of not knowing what you don't know.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

**Authentic voice and specificity**: Details like "pick and roll," the scrimmage scene, and "just stay out of the way" make this feel genuinely lived-in. You're not telling us he was unprepared—you're showing it.

**Smart structure**: This avoids the cliché "I failed, then I tried harder and succeeded" arc. Instead, the failure redirects him entirely, which feels more true to life and more interesting.

**Layered reflection**: The essay goes beyond surface-level learning. You move from "I wasn't good enough" to "casual vs. competitive play is different" to "you don't know what you don't know." That's genuine insight.

**Clean writing**: No grammar issues, good pacing, natural dialogue. The prose is unpretentious and readable.

## Room to Grow

**Emotional depth in the middle**: You tell us he was "really upset" but the scrimmage section moves pretty quickly. Lingering a bit more on how that felt—the embarrassment, the disappointment—would make the stakes feel higher.

**The debate pivot needs a touch more texture**: We learn he joined debate and was better at it, but a specific detail or two about what that actually *felt* like would strengthen this section. Did a particular moment make him realize he'd found his thing?

**The ending is solid but slightly expected**: "Failure is just information" is true and well-earned, but it's also a fairly common realization. Not a real problem, just worth noting.

## The Big Picture

This is genuinely good writing—clear, honest, well-paced, with real voice. The reflection is earned, not imposed. For someone returning to writing after decades, this shows real skill. Your dad should be proud, and keep writing.

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

prompt:
My friend wrote this essay and asked me for feedback. My dad started writing again at 70 — this is one of his first pieces in decades. Can you help me grade it? I'd like a grade out of 10 and brief feedback on what works and what doesn't. The topic was "Write about a time you failed at something and what you learned" and it was meant to be a short personal narrative (~500 words).

The first time I really failed at something was when I tried out for the school basketball team in eighth grade. I had been playing in the driveway all summer and I thought I was pretty good. My friend Marcus and I would play one-on-one for hours and I could beat him most of the time, so I figured making the team would be no problem.

The tryouts were three days long and I realized pretty quickly that I was out of my league. The other kids were faster and taller and they knew plays and formations that I had never even heard of. I didn't know what a "pick and roll" was. When the coach asked us to run a fast break drill, I had to watch other people do it first and I still got it wrong.

The worst part was the second day when we had to do a scrimmage. I got put on a team with kids I didn't know and I could tell they were annoyed with me because I kept being in the wrong position. One kid said "just stay out of the way" and I pretty much did for the rest of the game. I barely touched the ball.

When the list went up on Friday, my name wasn't on it. Obviously. Marcus made it though, which was hard because I thought we were at the same level. Turns out playing in your driveway and playing on an actual team are totally different things.

I was really upset for a while. I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did. I thought about trying out again the next year but I didn't. Instead I joined the debate team, which sounds like a weird pivot but it was actually good for me. I found out I was better at arguing than dribbling.

Looking back I think what I learned is that being okay at something in a casual setting doesn't mean you're actually good at it. There's a difference between doing something for fun and doing it competitively. I also learned that failing at one thing can push you toward something else that ends up being a better fit. I never would have tried debate if I had made the basketball team, and debate ended up being one of the best experiences of high school.

I still think about that kid who told me to stay out of the way sometimes. It was mean but he wasn't wrong. I was in over my head and I couldn't see it because I didn't have enough experience to know what good actually looked like. That's probably the biggest lesson — you don't know what you don't know until you put yourself in a situation where it becomes obvious.

So yeah, I failed at basketball tryouts. It felt terrible at the time but it worked out fine. I think everyone needs at least one experience like that to understand that failure isn't the end of anything, it's just information.

# Grade: 8/10

## What Works Really Well

**Authentic voice.** This reads like a real person reflecting honestly—"I told my mom I didn't care but she knew I did" and the lingering memory of that kid telling him to stay out of the way feel genuinely felt, not manufactured.

**Specific, concrete details.** He doesn't just say "I wasn't good"—we see him not knowing what a pick-and-roll is, watching others do the fast break drill, getting told to stay out of the way. These details make it vivid.

**Layered learning.** The reflection goes beyond "I learned I wasn't ready." He identifies that casual competence ≠ real competence, that failure redirected him somewhere better, and most insightfully—that you can't judge your own skill level without context. That's mature thinking.

**Good narrative structure.** Clear setup → challenge → concrete low point (the scrimmage) → consequence → reflection.

## What Could Be Stronger

**A bit short.** At roughly 420 words, it's under the ~500-word target. Could use 80-100 more words.

**The debate pivot feels rushed.** He mentions joining debate but doesn't show us what that was like. Did he struggle initially? What was the moment he realized he was better at it? More development here would strengthen the essay.

**Some reflection could be shown rather than told.** "There's a difference between casual and competitive play" is true, but showing it through a specific example would hit harder.

**Slight cliché ending.** The closing sentence is solid but a touch greeting-card-ish. The fresher insights come earlier in the reflection.

## Bottom Line

Your dad has a clear voice and good instincts for storytelling. The essay does exactly what the assignment asks—it's engaging, honest, and reflective. Just needs a bit more space to breathe and develop some of the middle sections. This is genuinely encouraging work for someone returning to writing.
